segments.conditions: The Sunset Route ran west through the Louisiana bayou country and across the Sabine River into East Texas. Segregated coaches were enforced through both states, and the Gulf Coast humidity made the un-air-conditioned cars stifling in summer months.

The segment through South Texas passed through ranch country where Mexican and Mexican-American laborers had long worked the rail lines themselves. San Antonio served as a major junction where passengers heading to different California destinations could transfer to connecting routes.

The long crossing of West Texas was arid, monotonous, and brutally hot, with stops at remote desert towns where passengers could step out briefly to stretch. El Paso was the major border crossing point where Mexican immigrants entered the US rail network, many heading to agricultural work in California.

Through the Chihuahuan and Sonoran deserts, the train passed through some of the most desolate landscape in North America. Summer temperatures inside the cars could exceed 100 degrees, and the railroad's Harvey House restaurants at stops like Deming provided the only reliable meals.

segments.cost: included in through-ticket; total New Orleans to LA approximately $25-$40 (1930s-1940s)
segments.conditions: The final stretch crossed the Colorado River at Yuma and climbed through the Imperial Valley into the Los Angeles Basin. For Black migrants from Texas and Louisiana, arriving at LA's Union Station meant entering a city with no legal segregation but deep informal racial boundaries in housing and employment.

segments.distance: approximately 380 miles (Coast Route or Valley Route)
segments.conditions: The northbound connection carried migrants to the San Francisco Bay Area, where Oakland's shipyards and the Port of San Francisco offered wartime and postwar industrial jobs. Oakland's Seventh Street became the cultural center of a Black community built largely by Southern Pacific arrivals from Texas and Louisiana.

who_used_it: Great Migration Phase 2 (1940s-1970s) — Black migrants from Texas and Louisiana heading to Los Angeles and Oakland for defense industry and postwar manufacturing jobs. Dust Bowl refugees from Oklahoma and Texas (1930s). Mexican immigrants entering at El Paso and heading to California agricultural work. WWII military personnel traveling to West Coast bases.
narrative_hook: In {year}, {ancestor_name} likely rode the Southern Pacific's Sunset Route from {origin} to {destination}, following the southern transcontinental corridor that carried tens of thousands of wartime and postwar migrants from the Gulf South to the factories and shipyards of California.
source_references: Richard J. Orsi, 'Sunset Limited: The Southern Pacific Railroad and the Development of the American West, 1850-1930' (University of California Press, 2005)
Isabel Wilkerson, 'The Warmth of Other Suns: The Epic Story of America's Great Migration' (Random House, 2010)
Quintard Taylor, 'In Search of the Racial Frontier: African Americans in the American West, 1528-1990' (W.W. Norton, 1998)
Gretchen Lemke-Santangelo, 'Abiding Courage: African American Migrant Women and the East Bay Community' (University of North Carolina Press, 1996)
